paper_title: Mid-Training with Self-Generated Data Improves Reinforcement Learning in Language Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: The effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in Large Language Models (LLMs) depends on the nature and diversity of the data used before and during RL. In particular, reasoning problems can often be approached in multiple ways that rely on different forms of reasoning, and exposure to only a limited range of such approaches in the training data may limit the effectiveness of RL. Motivated by this, we investigate using diverse self-generated data during mid-training as an intermediate step before RL training. Specifically, we adopt a bootstrapped data-generation framework guided by George Polya's problem-solving approaches for generating multiple variants of correct answers for each question in the training data, and then perform fine-tuning. We first provide a theoretical perspective on how mid-training on such data improves RL and explain how policy-gradient updates can incentivize combining multiple approaches. We then empirically demonstrate that RL-trained models initialized with our mid-training data achieve consistent improvements across various mathematical reasoning benchmarks and other OOD tasks like code generation and narrative reasoning. Overall, our investigative study shows that a language model learning multiple problem-solving approaches, through self-generated data helps subsequent RL.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has recently emerged as an effective technique for improving the performance of LLMs, particularly on complex reasoning tasks. By encouraging longer reasoning chains, RL enables performance gains through inference-time scaling. Such reasoning chains often display behaviors, including multi-step reasoning, self-verification, and self-correction, that resemble human-like problem-solving [21, 11, 26]. However, recent works argue that RL with simple, verifiable rewards (RLVR) primarily performs distributional sharpening [65, 14]. It selectively amplifies behaviors already present in the base model rather than inducing genuinely new capabilities [60]. In some cases, RLVR can even degrade performance under pass@k evaluation absent intervention [7, 52]. This highlights that RL effectiveness is closely tied to what the model learned prior to RL. Recent studies show that RL struggles to elicit self-reflective behaviors in Llama-3.2 family models that lack strong priors [9, 42]. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [62] demonstrate that capability gains beyond the base model are achievable only under specific conditions on both the task and the training data. For reasoning tasks, this observation is particularly relevant. single problem can often be solved in multiple valid ways, relying on different forms of reasoning. When model lacks exposure to diverse approaches, subsequent RL training may struggle to improve its performance. Together, these observations highlight the central role of training data in shaping model capabilities and motivate the need for mid-training ( 4) as practical means of introducing such data, enabling subsequent RL training to operate over richer priors. Correspondence to: aravik13@asu.edu Work done prior to joining Google DeepMind. Preprint. In this work, we investigate how mid-training with diverse self-generated data can be simple and effective way to improve RL in language models. Specifically, we study simple setting in which, for each question, we generate multiple diverse, correct solutions and use them to perform fine-tuning as mid-training step. In our setup, training data is generated directly from the same base model that later undergoes this mid-training stage, enabling self-improvement from its own generated reasoning without relying on distillation from stronger models. We adopt bootstrapped syntheticdata generation framework that systematically produces diverse yet correct reasoning trajectories by conditioning on explicit problem-solving heuristics. Inspired by the classical problem-solving approaches of George Pólya [40], we construct heuristic-guided few-shot prompts that explicitly encourage the model to generate responses with distinct reasoning approaches for the same underlying question. By filtering these responses for correctness and adherence to the targeted heuristic, we obtain multiple diverse solution variants per question without requiring human annotation or supervision from stronger models. Using these self-generated reasoning trajectories, we fine-tune the base model. Our experiments show that applying GRPO-based RL to mid-trained models consistently improves performance across range of mathematical reasoning benchmarks, with gains often increasing as the diversity of reasoning approaches introduced during mid-training grows. Compared to vanilla RL baselines, models mid-trained with larger number of heuristics achieve higher pass@k, particularly at larger values of k. At pass@64, this corresponds to improvements of 2.85% on MATH-500, 5.7% on AIME 2024, 6.55% on AIME 2025, 2.26% on HMMT 2025, 6.34% on AMC 2023, and 3.17% on OlympiadBench. While the optimal number of heuristics varies across datasets, models trained with higher reasoning diversity consistently outperform both vanilla RL and STaR across all benchmarks. Additionally, we provide theoretical perspective that explains how mid-training with multiple approaches helps RL and explain how it incentivizes combining reasoning strategies in single response. This compositional behavior is empirically observed in our analysis in 6.1 and aligns with findings from recent research [59, 5]. Our additional analysis shows that, under fixed midtraining budget, learning multiple problem-solving approaches per question yields greater benefits than learning single approach across larger number of questions. We further show that our heuristic-guided self-generated data is more diverse than data distilled from stronger teacher model (Vendi Score 13.81 vs. 10.95 for QwQ-32B distillation), leading to improved downstream pass@1 and pass@64 performance. Finally, despite Pólyas heuristics having math-centric focus, we observe that the resulting problem-solving strategies generalize beyond math, yielding considerable gains on out-of-domain benchmarks, including around code generation and narrative reasoning.

sections.title: 2 Related Works
sections.content: Synthetic Data Generation large body of work uses LLMs to generate synthetic data without human annotation [18, 13, 56]. Earlier work focused on generating training datasets for instruction tuning [31, 54]; Wang et al. [53] proposes bootstrapping framework in which pre-trained model iteratively generates instruction-response pairs to improve its instruction-following ability. Other works [34, 32] improve the reasoning capabilities of smaller models by distilling synthetic data from larger models. Zelikman et al. [61] introduces self-training approach in which model fine-tunes on its own generated rationales, and Setlur et al. [45] shows that using both correct and incorrect synthetic traces improves RL sample efficiency on math reasoning. Puri et al. [39] show that increasing instruction variation yields larger gains than scaling the number of training instances. Our work differs from these prior approaches by generating multiple response variations per question via heuristic-conditioned prompting and studying the effect of this diversity during mid-training on subsequent RL. Reinforcement Learning for LLMs Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) [38] has become standard post-training step, aligning models with human preferences by training reward model and optimizing the policy with algorithms such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) [44]. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [41] reframes preference alignment as supervised objective and matches or exceeds PPO-based methods, with several variants further modifying the training objective [25, 43, 1, 30]. Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) [26, 16, 12] has since driven substantial gains on challenging reasoning tasks. However, RL is more effective when applied to base models with strong priors [42, 9, 62], and several works [36] distill task-specific reasoning data before RL. Yuan et al. [59] further show that RL enables models to learn novel 2 compositions of atomic skills not explicitly taught during prior training. Our work differs by midtraining on diverse self-generated responses produced via Pólya-style problem-solving heuristics, and theoretically analyzing how subsequent RL combines these approaches.

sections.title: 3 Preliminaries
sections.content: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). SFT trains pre-trained LLM πθ on dataset of questions and their corresponding responses. Let DSFT = {(x, y)} denote the training dataset, where is question and = (y1, y2, . . . , yT ) is the corresponding response sequence. SFT minimizes the negative log-likelihood of the responses in DSFT, LSFT(θ) = E(x,y)DSFT (cid:35) (cid:0)yt y<t, x(cid:1) . log πθ (cid:34) (cid:88) t=1 In RL, the language model πθ serves as policy that defines conditional RL via Policy Gradients. distribution over output tokens given question and the previously generated tokens. Let DRL denote dataset of questions used for RL. For given question DRL, the model generates response = (y1, . . . , yT ) autoregressively by sampling tokens according to πθ(yt y<t, x). At each time step t, the state is (x, y<t) and the action is the next token yt, so complete response constitutes trajectory sampled from the policy. The objective is to maximize the expected reward of trajectories generated for questions in DRL, with gradient θJ(θ) = ExDRL, yπθ (x) [A(x, y) θ log πθ(y x)] , where A(x, y) is an advantage function that measures the relative quality of response under the reward signal. Among commonly used approaches such as REINFORCE [55] and PPO [44], we employ Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [46] in our experiments due to its simplicity.

sections.title: 4 Mid-Training (MT)
sections.content: Prior works [33, 50] characterize mid-training as an intermediate phase between pre-training and post-training, in which base model is further trained on high-quality or task-focused data to induce targeted improvements while preserving existing capabilities. We focus on mid-training with diverse self-generated data and study its role in improving subsequent RL training. Specifically, we construct mid-training dataset in which each question is associated with multiple correct solution trajectories, exposing the model to valid ways of solving the same problem during supervised training. This setup lets us analyze how the guided diversity introduced during mid-training influences both RL performance and the reasoning approaches observed during the RL phase. Heuristic-Guided Data Construction To systematically introduce diverse ways of solving the same problem, we draw inspiration from the problem-solving heuristics described in How to Solve It by George Pólya [40]. This work presents structured view of problem solving, emphasizing that single problem can often be approached through multiple valid strategies, such as working backward, introducing auxiliary quantities, or decomposing the problem into simpler subproblems. comprehensive list of heuristic descriptions and examples is provided in A.11. i=1 Formally, we associate each heuristic with textual description Dh and small set of few-shot exemplars Eh = {(xi, rh,i, ai)}4 i=1. Given an existing dataset of questions and answers Dseed = {(xi, ai)}Dseed , our goal is to generate, for each question x, set of solution trajectories that reflect distinct reasoning behaviors. To this end, for each questionheuristic pair (x, h), we prompt the base model π with Dh and Eh and sample multiple candidate responses. The sampled trajectories are then filtered and refined to ensure both correctness and adherence to the targeted heuristic. We first apply rule-based verifiers (e.g., Math-Verify) to retain responses that produce the correct final answer. The remaining candidates are scored using reward model Rϕ, which evaluates how well each response follows the intended heuristic given and Dh. For each questionheuristic pair, we select the response with the highest reward score as the final heuristicspecific solution trajectory. The resulting collection constitutes the mid-training data. Training on this dataset differs from standard SFT in that each question is paired with multiple distinct solution trajectories rather than single correct response. Bottom left: The resulting dataset DPólya, containing solution variants per question, is used to mid-train the base-instruct model before RL. of heuristic-specific trajectories associated with question x. The mid-training objective is the average negative log-likelihood over these trajectories, LMT(θ) = E(x,Yx) (cid:34) 1 (cid:88) Ti(cid:88) i=1 t=1 log πθ (cid:0)y(i) (cid:35) <t, x(cid:1) y(i) . Optimizing this objective encourages the model to assign non-negligible probability to several next tokens at prefixes shared across different solution strategies, each corresponding to distinct reasoning behavior. As result, mid-trained models can exhibit next-token distributions with multiple high-probability modes at such branching points, in contrast to models trained on single solution per question. In the following section, we analyze how these n-modal next-token distributions affect policy-gradient updates during RL. 4.1 Theory Theorem 4.1 Let πθ(. x, y<t) = softmax(zπ(x, y<t)) be an auto-regressive language model parameterized by θ updated via single policy gradient step with learning rate η and advantage for sampled token yt V. Key insight. The above theorem highlights how single policy-gradient update affects the probability distribution of language model. We consider two regimes: uni-modal: when model is too confident on single token and N-modal: when model has high probability on tokens. For the purposes of this discussion, we assume that in the -modal regime probability mass is distributed across << dominant tokens, each with probability 1ϵ , although in practice these probabilities need not be uniform. Implicitly, our mid-training approach incentivizes such N-modal next-token distributions as discussed in the previous section. From the theorem, we see that under uni-modal regime, the induced change in the sampled tokens probability scales as ϵ2. Consequently, once the model becomes highly confident in its prediction, single policy-gradient update produces only negligible change in the output distribution. In contrast, under the N-modal regime, the sampled tokens probability scales as 1 , which leads to conservative yet appreciable changes. As increases, 4 the changes become more conservative, preventing it from committing to single mode within few update steps. This prevents mode collapse, as discussed in recent work [60]. Proposition 4.2 Consider the -modal regime in which probability mass 1 ϵ is distributed among dominant modes. Under single policy-gradient update with negative advantage < 0, the probability mass removed from the sampled token yt is redistributed predominantly to the remaining 1 dominant modes. Discussion Proposition 4.2 highlights how probability mass is redistributed during single negative policy gradient update. This result provides conceptual explanation for why exposing the model to diverse approaches to solving problem can be beneficial. In particular, when multiple highprobability continuations exist at given prefix, the sampled token corresponds to one specific continuation, while other tokens correspond to alternative plausible continuations (i.e., different problem-solving approaches). negative update reduces the probability of the sampled token and reallocates mass toward these alternatives. As result, the model learns to combine different problemsolving approaches into single response during RL training, as shown in A.10. As the model is exposed to more problem-solving approaches, it increasingly learns to combine elements of these approaches within single response, through the course of RL training.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: 5.1 Experimental Setup Baselines In our investigation, we compare our models against zero-shot baseline and STaR [61]. The zero-shot baseline evaluates the base model without any additional training. For experiments involving RL, we compare mid-trained models against two baselines. The first is vanilla RL baseline in which GRPO is applied directly to the base model. The second is STaR+RL, where GRPO is applied after STaR-based fine-tuning. Training Datasets For mid-training, we use the GSM8K [6] training set, which contains 7,473 questionsanswer pairs and serves as the seed dataset Dseed. Following the procedure described in 4, we obtain filtered set of 7,112 questions, each paired with up to 64 heuristic-specific variants of the correct solution. We construct multiple mid-training datasets with {1, 2, 4, . . . , 64} solution variants per question and train separate model on each mid-training dataset. Models and Reward We use Llama 3.23BInstruct [10] as the primary base model for all experiments, including both baselines and mid-training, and report additional results with Qwen2.5 7BInstruct in the A.5. We use Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B [29] as the reward model (Rϕ) to score responses during data generation. Evaluation Details We evaluate on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks: Math-500 [17], AIME 2024 [63], AIME 2025 [64], AMC 2023 [35], HMMT 2025 [2], and OlympiadBench [15], covering wide range of difficulties and reasoning types. We use Math-Verify [19] to verify the correctness of the models generated solutions automatically. Model performance is measured using the pass@k metric [23], where problem is solved if at least one of samples is correct. (cid:1)(cid:3), where is Following Chen et al. sections.title: 6 Analysis and Discussion
sections.content: 6.1 Analysis of Reasoning Traces The results discussed in the previous section indicate that mid-training on self-generated data with diverse problem-solving approaches improves subsequent RL. Our theoretical discussion in 4.1 offers insight into how these models might combine different ways of solving problem within single reasoning chain during RL. To study this effect, we analyze reasoning traces sampled from both RL and mid-trained models using an LLM-based judge (GPT-4o-mini; [20]) to identify problem-solving approaches in the generated solutions. The classifier is defined using few-shot prompts derived from 64 of the reasoning heuristics in Pólyas taxonomy, each consisting of heuristic description along with positive and negative examples (see Fig. 10). Given reasoning chain, the judge outputs binary decision for each heuristic. We apply this to chains sampled from mid-trained models trained with {2, 4, 8, 16} distinct heuristics per question and their RL-trained counterparts, randomly selecting one solution per problem from AIME 2024. Fig. 3a shows the fraction of reasoning chains that exhibit more than one problem-solving behavior. For mid-trained models without RL, this fraction remains relatively stable, whereas RL leads to more chains that combine multiple approaches, and the trend strengthens as the number of approaches introduced during mid-training increases. When = 16, the RL-trained model exhibits composition in 56.7% of sampled chains, compared to 23.3% for the corresponding mid-trained model. To better understand how these approaches are combined, Figure 3b lists the most common heuristic combinations observed for models trained with = 16. Such combinations are not explicitly demonstrated during mid-training, but emerge consistently after RL. This provides empirical evidence that RL combines problem-solving approaches introduced during mid-training, in line with our theoretical discussion and recent work [59, 5]. These findings raise an interesting question. Does RL 7 r A @ a . 40

We leave more detailed investigation to future work. 6.2 More Problems or More Approaches? To understand the effect of learning multiple problem-solving approaches, we conduct controlled experiment that fixes the total number of training instances during mid-training while varying the number of unique problems and the number of approaches per problem. More specifically, we ask: Does learning more problems or more problem-solving approaches during mid-training better benefit RL? To answer this question, we construct two mid-training datasets using self-generated solutions from the GSM8K training set. In setting 1, we sample single correct response from the base model for each question, resulting in dataset of 7,408 unique questionsolution pairs. In setting 2, we select 463 unique questions and sample 16 distinct problem-solving approaches for each, yielding total of 7,408 training instances. We then perform mid-training using each dataset, followed by RL. Fig. 4a compares the performance of the RL-trained models under the two mid-training regimes. We observe that mid-training with multiple problem-solving approaches per question (Setting 2) consistently outperforms mid-training on larger number of distinct problems with single solution each (Setting 1) across all values of k. Notably, this improvement persists despite both settings using the same total number of supervised training instances. This result suggests that learning multiple solving approaches, even when applied to smaller set of problems, provides an avg. relative improvement of 7% compared to mid-training on many problems with only one solution each. 6.3 Problem-Solving Approaches Without Correctness We examine whether exposing model to varied problem-solving approaches alone is sufficient to improve RL, or whether answer correctness is essential. We construct variant of our midtraining data using procedure in 4, but where the generated reasoning chains follow specific Pólya-style approaches yet yield incorrect final answers. This isolates the effect of exposure to different approaches from the effect of training on correct solutions. We mid-train the base model on this data for {2, 4, 8, 16} and apply RL with the same setup as in 5 and compare against vanilla RL training. From Figure 4b, we observe that when mid-training uses approaches that lead to incorrect answers, increasing does not improve RL performance. Instead, average pass@k accuracy decreases across all k, and these models fall below vanilla RL on all six benchmarks. RL therefore benefits from varied problem-solving approaches only when those approaches lead to correct final answers during mid-training. 8 6.4 Can Simple Distillation Produce Diverse Reasoning Approaches? To investigate whether distillation from strong reasoning teacher can provide diverse problemsolving approaches, for each question in DPólya we sample 16 reasoning chains from QwQ-32B [49], mid-train base instruct model on these traces, then apply RL. We compute the Vendi Score ( A.3) on the resulting dataset and compare against our = 16 midtrained RL model in Fig. 5. Fig. 5 shows that our mid-trained model achieves better pass@64 after RL and comparable or better pass@1 than the teacherdistillation baseline. The distillation dataset obtains Vendi Score of 10.95, compared to 13.81 for our mid-training dataset. The distilled models RL rollouts are also notably more verbose and repetitive, failure mode also reported by Li et al. [28]. These observations motivate the bootstrapping setup in 5 for our investigates, which aims to isolate the effect of learning multiple problem-solving approaches without confounding factors such as capability transfer or distillation efficiency from stronger teacher. 6.5 Generalization to Other Domains Vanilla RL Ours n=16 Distill+RL AIME 24 AIME 25 AMC HMMT 25 0 20 40 60 Score (%) Figure 5: Distill+RL vs. ours: p@1 () and p@64 () across math benchmarks. Our mid-training relies on Pólyas problem-solving heuristics, which are inherently math-centric. natural question is whether the resulting problem-solving approaches generalize to other domains. To investigate this, we evaluate on two out-of-domain benchmarks. For coding, we take our mid-trained models (n=32, 64) from 5 and perform RL training on the KodCode-Light-RL-10K [57], then evaluate on HumanEval [3], comparing against Vanilla RL (RL applied directly to the base-instruct model without mid-training). Additionally, we take our post-RL models (n=32, 64) and evaluate on MuSR [48], which targets long-context, multi-step reasoning over natural language narratives. Approach Table 2: Results on HumanEval and MuSR. From Table 2, we observe that our mid-trained models achieve better performance than the Vanilla RL baseline across both out-of-domain benchmarks. On HumanEval, our = 32 and = 64 models achieve 52.34% and 52.82%, respectively, compared to 51.14% for Vanilla RL. We also observe larger gains on MuSR. On the Murder Mystery sub-task, our models achieve 56.94% and 57.36%, compared to 53.15% for Vanilla RL. In addition to this, on Team Allocations, the Vanilla RL baseline (23.46%) underperforms even the base instruct model (25.70%), while our = 32 and = 64 models achieve 39.07% and 38.57% respectively. These results indicate that the diverse problem-solving approaches induced during mid-training can also generalize to other domains, with the largest gains on tasks that require multi-step reasoning. 25.70 0.18 45.25 0.04 23.46 0.14 46.23 0.24 45.98 0.20 38.57 0.05 47.08 0.58 39.07 0.44 50.50 0.07 41.84 0.14 51.14 0.04 53.15 0.42 52.82 0.09 57.36 0.15 56.94 0.18 52.34 0.14 Base Instruct Vanilla RL n=64 (RL) n=32 (RL) Placements Allocations Murder Mystery HumanEval Object MuSR Team

sections.title: 7 Conclusions
sections.content: In this paper, we investigated mid-training strategy to improve RL in LLMs using diverse selfgenerated data. For each training question, we systematically generated multiple correct solution trajectories guided by George Pólyas problem-solving heuristics and performed fine-tuning before RL. We showed that this improves the performance of subsequent RL across range of mathematical reasoning benchmarks, especially at higher pass@k values. We provided theoretical perspective on how such training benefits RL and can incentivize the composition of diverse problem-solving approaches. Our analysis of reasoning traces provides empirical evidence for this. Furthermore, our analysis reveals that, under fixed mid-training budget, learning multiple problem-solving approaches per question is more beneficial for RL than increasing the number of distinct training problems with only single self-generated solution.

sections.title: A Appendix
sections.content: A.1 Limitations The problem-solving heuristics we use are drawn from Pólyas How to Solve It and are inherently math-centric; while our HumanEval and MuSR results (6.5) suggest the resulting approaches transfer beyond mathematics, fully domain-general taxonomy remains an open question. That said, such diverse approaches plausibly already exist within large-scale pre-training corpora, so our mid-training procedure can be viewed as surfacing and consolidating behaviors the base model has likely been exposed to, rather than introducing fundamentally new ones, view consistent with the open question raised in 6.1 about whether RL gives rise to genuinely emergent behaviors or primarily composes capabilities acquired during pre-training. Although our mid-trained models achieve consistent gains at higher pass@k, their pass@1 is only slightly better than the vanilla RL and STaR baselines; the focus of our study is not to achieve state-of-the-art single-sample accuracy, but to understand how exposure to multiple problem-solving approaches per question shapes the reasoning behavior elicited by subsequent RL and improve its performance. A.2 Broader Impact Our work contributes to the broader effort of understanding the mechanisms underlying RL training in language models. Rather than proposing RL as black-box technique for capability gains, we study how the data model is exposed to prior to RL shapes the behaviors that RL subsequently elicits, and provide both theoretical and empirical evidence that exposure to multiple problem-solving approaches per question encourages RL to compose these approaches within single reasoning chain. Improved understanding of these mechanisms can inform more principled and predictable post-training pipelines, reduce reliance on trial-and-error scaling of RL compute, and contribute to ongoing discussions about whether RL induces genuinely emergent behaviors or primarily recombines capabilities acquired during pre-training. We do not foresee direct negative societal impacts beyond those generally associated with improved reasoning in language models. A.3 Diversity of the Mid-Training Dataset To quantify the diversity of heuristic-guided reasoning traces in our mid-training dataset, we use the Vendi Score [8], similarity-based metric designed to measure the diversity of collection. The Vendi Score computes the eigenspectrum of pairwise similarity matrix and returns the effective number of dissimilar elements in the collection, ranging from 1, when all items are identical, to n, the number of items in the collection. We calculate the Vendi Score at the question level by first embedding all 462,280 questionresponse pairs (7,112 questions 65 heuristics) using the text-embedding-3-small model [37]. Since each embedding encodes both the shared question and the heuristic-specific response, we subtract the per-question mean embedding to isolate the variation attributable to the response. We then construct the cosine similarity kernel and compute the Vendi Score as we increase the number of heuristics from 1 to 64. Table 3 reports the Vendi Score for different values of n, along with the corresponding post-RL performance. From the Table 3, we observe that as increases, the Vendi Score also increases, indicating that the heuristic-guided data creation mechanism introduces more diverse reasoning approaches. However, the Vendi Score does not reach n, as some overlap between problem-solving strategies is expected, such as between analogy-based and auxiliary-problem strategies. We also observe that downstream gains are not strictly monotonic with respect to the Vendi Score, although the top-performing models tend to have higher Vendi Scores. This suggests that learning from more diverse problem-solving approaches can be helpful. A.4 Training Details In this section, we provide the details of our training setup. All of experiments are conducted on 4NVIDIA H100 GPUs. Mid-Training & STaR Setup. We implement our mid-training using the trl [51] framework. All models are optimized with the AdamW optimizer under cosine learning rate schedule, with 16 Table 3: Vendi Score and downstream RL performance as the number of problem-solving heuristics varies. Vendi Score Avg. pass@1 Avg. pass@64 Vanilla RL 2 4 8 16 32 64 N/A 1.99 3.87 7.44 13.81 24.16 40.56 16.87 17.11 17.18 16.75 18.35 17.32 17.91 44.22 43.04 43.97 44.09 48.09 45.21 47. the initial learning rate set to 1.0 106 and warmup ratio of 0.1. We enable bfloat16 mixedprecision training and gradient checkpointing to reduce memory usage, and set the maximum sequence length to 5190 tokens. To ensure fair comparison across different mid-trained models, we train all configurations for single epoch with fixed number of optimization step i.e., 444 steps. This is achieved by adjusting the effective batch size according to the number of heuristic variations n, as summarized in Table 4. For STaR, we first perform inference over Dseed (i.e., the GSM8K training set) with temperature 0 and filter the correct reasoning chains. For questions that are answered incorrectly by the model, we perform rationalization and add the generated reasoning chains to construct DSTaR, which contains approximately 7.21k problemrationale instances. Number of heuristics (n) Effective batch size 64 32 16 8 4 2 1 1024 512 256 128 64 32 16 Table 4: Effective batch size used for mid-training with different numbers of heuristic variations. Reinforcement Learning. We perform RL training with DAPO-Math-17k [58] dataset using the GRPO algorithm in the verl [47] framework. To accelerate rollout generation and evaluation, we employ vLLM [24]. During training, prompts are truncated to maximum length of 1024 tokens and responses are capped at 3072 tokens. We sample 16 responses per prompt using temperature of 1.0. Training is conducted with batch size of 32 and mini-batch size of 8, i.e., one on-policy update and three off-policy updates per optimization step. The model is optimized using the AdamW optimizer with constant learning rate of 1.0 106, and gradient checkpointing is enabled to reduce memory usage. We apply asymmetric clipping with ϵlow = 0.2 and ϵhigh = 0.28. Recent work [22] suggest removing the KL regularization term, as it allows less restrictive model updates while showing no significant evidence of training instability or overoptimization; accordingly, we do not use KL regularization during training. Rewards are based on final answer correctness, and all RL experiments are run for 1 epoch. A.5 Experiments on Qwen2.57BInstruct We conduct additional experiments on Qwen2.57BInstruct model, with the same mid-training procedure as discussed in 4&5. During subsequent RL training, we sample 8 responses per prompt using temperature 1.0. We discuss the results of our study below. From Table 5, we observe that the trends in the performance of the mid-trained models differ from those on Llama 3.23BInstruct. On Qwen2.57BInstruct, the zero-shot baseline achieves 17 Table 5: Mid-Training Results. Pass@1 and pass@64 performance on four mathematical reasoning benchmarks for Qwen2.57BInstruct. DPólya denotes mid-training on GSM8K using self-generated, diverse, heuristic-guided solution variants, with variants per question. Results are reported as mean standard error over three runs. Best and second-best results are bolded and underlined. Methods Reasoning Benchmarks (pass@1 / pass@64) AVG. AIME 24 AIME AMC 23 HMMT 25 Zero-Shot N/A 10.830.03 / 42.711.01 7.290.05 / 42.120.40 51.580.09 / 92.660. 4.160.02 / 28.060.41 18.47 / 51.39 DSTaR DPólya N/A 7.700.01 / 37.390. 6.240.10 / 41.630.35 45.810.09 / 88.000.36 2.000.02 / 28.840.45 15.44 / 48.97 4 16 32 7.580.02 / 36.460.31 4.140.08 / 37.260.29 41.460.06 / 91.350.46 1.390.01 / 26.020. 13.64 / 47.77 9.680.03 / 40.900.59 5.910.04 / 41.610.87 48.950.09 / 92.880.28 3.680.04 / 26.620.27 17.06 / 50. 9.460.08 / 41.630.36 5.570.04 / 39.590.34 48.410.05 / 92.590.26 3.600.03 / 26.520.31 16.76 / 50.08 9.740.02 / 41.580. 5.760.04 / 39.360.29 48.520.03 / 92.480.32 3.610.05 / 25.550.46 16.91 / 49.74 the highest average pass@1 (18.47%) and pass@64 (51.39%), suggesting that this stronger base model already exhibits substantial reasoning diversity. STaR underperforms zero-shot on average (15.44% / 48.97%), though it achieves the best pass@64 on HMMT 2025 (28.84%). Among Pólya configurations, = 8 achieves the strongest average performance (17.06% / 50.50%) and outperforms zero-shot on AMC 2023 pass@64 (92.88% vs. 92.66%).
